facts.fact_text: A general dissimilarity coefficient due to Gower (1971) handles all attribute types. For each attribute, a dissimilarity dj(xij, xi′j) is computed, and these are combined into an overall dissimilarity.
d(x_i, x_{i'}) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{p} w_{ij} d_j(x_{ij}, x_{i'j})}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} w_{ij}}
For each attribute, a dissimilarity dj(xij, xi′j) is computed, and these are combined into an overall dissimilarity.
Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X).
In some applications the features are not used at all. Rather, the user supplies an N × N matrix of pairwise dissimilarities among the observations, based on domain knowledge.
The goal is to directly infer the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.

Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of observations.
Combinatorial algorithms work directly with the observed data or pairwise dissimilarities. They produce a partition of the data, assigning each observation to one and only one cluster.
Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X).
One must settle for estimating rather crude global models, such as Gaussian mixtures or various simple descriptive statistics that characterize Pr(X).

With supervised learning there is a clear measure of success, or lack thereof, that can be used to judge adequacy in particular situations and to compare the effectiveness of different methods over various situations.
Mixture modeling assumes that the data are generated by a mixture of underlying probability distributions. Each component distribution represents a cluster.
f(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k f_k(x)

Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of observations. Mixture modeling has a similar goal.
One must settle for estimating rather crude global models, such as Gaussian mixtures or various simple descriptive statistics that characterize Pr(X).

Mode seeking (‘bump hunting’) methods take a nonparametric perspective, attempting to directly estimate distinct modes of the probability density function. Observations ‘closest’ to each respective mode then define the individual clusters.
Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X).
In low-dimensional problems (say p ≤3), there are a variety of effective nonparametric methods for directly estimating the density Pr(X) itself at all X-values, and representing it graphically.
Owing to the curse of dimensionality, these methods fail in high dimensions.

Hierarchical clustering produces a nested sequence of partitions, from single observation clusters at the bottom to one cluster containing all observations at the top. A dendrogram represents this hierarchy.

Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of observations.
The goal is to directly infer the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.
Agglomerative (bottom-up) clustering starts with each observation in its own cluster. The two closest clusters are merged, and this is repeated until there is only one cluster.

Hierarchical clustering produces a nested sequence of partitions, from single observation clusters at the bottom to one cluster containing all observations at the top. A dendrogram represents this hierarchy.
Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X).
Divisive (top-down) clustering starts with all observations in one cluster. The cluster is split into two, and this is repeated recursively until each observation is in its own cluster.

Hierarchical clustering produces a nested sequence of partitions, from single observation clusters at the bottom to one cluster containing all observations at the top. A dendrogram represents this hierarchy.
Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X).

Single linkage (nearest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity between two groups as the minimum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other: d_SL(G, H) = min_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
d_{SL}(G, H) = \min_{i \in G, i' \in H} d_{ii'}
Agglomerative (bottom-up) clustering starts with each observation in its own cluster. The two closest clusters are merged, and this is repeated until there is only one cluster.
Complete linkage (furthest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity as the maximum dissimilarity: d_CL(G, H) = max_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
Complete linkage (furthest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity as the maximum dissimilarity: d_CL(G, H) = max_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
d_{CL}(G, H) = \max_{i \in G, i' \in H} d_{ii'}

Single linkage (nearest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity between two groups as the minimum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other: d_SL(G, H) = min_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
Group average defines the dissimilarity as the average of all pairwise dissimilarities: d_GA(G, H) = (1/(N_G × N_H)) ∑_{i∈G} ∑_{i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
Group average defines the dissimilarity as the average of all pairwise dissimilarities: d_GA(G, H) = (1/(N_G × N_H)) ∑_{i∈G} ∑_{i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
d_{GA}(G, H) = \frac{1}{N_G N_H} \sum_{i \in G} \sum_{i' \in H} d_{ii'}

Single linkage (nearest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity between two groups as the minimum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other: d_SL(G, H) = min_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
Complete linkage (furthest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity as the maximum dissimilarity: d_CL(G, H) = max_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.

Ward's method uses an ANOVA-based approach that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares. At each step, merge the pair of clusters that leads to the minimum increase in total within-cluster variance.
Agglomerative (bottom-up) clustering starts with each observation in its own cluster. The two closest clusters are merged, and this is repeated until there is only one cluster.
Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X).
Group average defines the dissimilarity as the average of all pairwise dissimilarities: d_GA(G, H) = (1/(N_G × N_H)) ∑_{i∈G} ∑_{i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
Each of the 512 × 512 pixel blocks (or points) is approximated by its closest cluster centroid, known as a codeword.
The clustering process is called the encoding step, and the collection of centroids is called the codebook.
More generally a rate/distortion curve would be used to assess the tradeoff.
For example, tree-structured VQ finds the centroids with a top-down, 2-means style algorithm, as alluded to in Section 14.3.12. This allows successive refinement of the compression.

Using Shannon coding theory, we know that in general a variable length code will do better, and the rate then becomes −∑K ℓ=1 pℓlog2(pℓ)/4. The term in the numerator is the entropy of the distribution pℓ of the codewords in the image.

\text{rate} = -\sum_{\ell=1}^{K} p_\ell \log_2(p_\ell) / 4

More generally a rate/distortion curve would be used to assess the tradeoff.
Divisive (top-down) clustering starts with all observations in one cluster. The cluster is split into two, and this is repeated recursively until each observation is in its own cluster.

K-medoids clustering is a variant of K-means where the cluster representatives are restricted to be observations in the dataset. For each cluster, find the observation that minimizes the total distance to other points in that cluster.
m_k = \arg\min_{i \in C_k} \sum_{i' \in C_k} d(x_i, x_{i'})
Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X).
In some applications the features are not used at all. Rather, the user supplies an N × N matrix of pairwise dissimilarities among the observations, based on domain knowledge.

The silhouette coefficient s(i) for observation i is defined as s(i) = (b(i) - a(i))/max{a(i), b(i)}, where a(i) is the average dissimilarity of i to all other objects in the same cluster, and b(i) is the minimum average dissimilarity of i to objects in other clusters.
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}

Cluster analysis attempts to find multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of observations.
